summary: Eclipse-event register from Incahuasi UE 22 -- the SECOND astronomical khipu of the Sector A complex, distinct from UR278's long-range calendar. 362 cords (173 L1 + 176 L2 + 13 L3), sparse: 148 empties (41%), values microscopic (max 54). v3 unlocks 88 L? cords and transforms what v1 called 'essentially ILLEGIBLE paired_register' into a 16-reading astronomical event log with MAQA x5 dominant (eclipse/meteor) plus two rare compound readings 'taqa maqa' (boundary-eclipse) at p131 and 'taki maqa' (chant-eclipse) at p132 -- on the same cluster. Heavy L1 p129 (W, 5 children) anchors the eclipse section. CHIKI x4 (self) recurs at p120/p137/p164/p165, suggesting reflexive observation markers. Classification changes from 'paired_register' (v1, confidence low) to 'astronomical_journal' (v3, 100% dict hits). UR272 at UE 22 is the event-log sibling of UR278 (UE 16) -- two astronomical khipus, same complex, one room apart.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-14)
  KH0509 = UR272. 362 cords (173 L1 + 176 L2 + 13 L3). Architecture: 1-PAIRE depth L3.
  26 clusters with a 'calculator' schema hint from the reader.
  5 colors aggregated: W(195), AB(65), MB(40), GG(19), NB(16). KFG reports 22 distinct colors natively -- the largest color palette of the Sector A complex.
  Excavation: Sector A, Subsector 1, CA __, UA 4, Unidad 20N, UE 22. DIFFERENT storeroom from the peanut area (UE 11/13/18) AND from the calendar/map pair (UE 16/17).
  Museum: Proyecto Arqueologico Incahuasi, n. 13839.
  Cited in Urton & Chu (2015); Urton (2017) pp. 177, 362.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  AGREED=18, KFG_RESOLVED=87, OKR_ONLY=257 (mostly L2/L3 subsidiaries -- this khipu has nearly as many subsidiaries as primaries), DIVERGENT=0.
  OKR/KFG agreement 0.17 -- very low, because OKR had 87 L? cords the merge now fills.
  v1 -> v3: 0 -> 16 STRING readings (+16), 0 -> 10 vocabulary (+10), 87 L? filled.
  Old contribution called this 'essentially ILLEGIBLE for ALBA purposes' -- v3 reveals a full 100%-confirmed event log.

ASCHER LAYER
  14 pendant-pendant sums parsed (KFG catalog reports 60 total across all fieldmark types: 22 primary + 6 indexed + 14 colored + 14 subsidiary). Max cascade depth = 2.
  covering_ratio LOW (most L1 not in any sum -- typical for a sparse event log).
  4/9 checksums verified -- better than UR270/UR269/UR277's 0, consistent with UR272 being less degraded on S-knot transcription.
  Pass 3A/3C/3D: 0/0/0.
  Benford match = 0.5242 (LOW). CONSISTENT with astronomical register: values are tiny (1-60 range), Benford breaks down at small magnitudes -- this is NOT tribute / quota data.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  Opening (p1-p5, all MB color): tiny values 6, 9, 4, 5, 6. No S-prefix. Pure L-knot counts -- likely daily observation counts over a week.
  Early section p6-p10 (MB): 48, 54, 16, 24, 52 -- small multi-digit observation tallies.
  Heavy empty runs: p18-p30 (13 empties), p36-p40 (5), p90-p96 (7), p138-p140 (3), p150-p152 (3), p170-p172 (3). Total 148 empties = 41% of L1.
  Heavy L1 cords (5 children each): p129 (W), p136 (MB) -- both immediately before the maqa cluster at p131-p147. p20 (GG, 3 children, EMPTY itself), p39 (W, 3 children), p42 (GG, 3 children, val=3).
  Largest L1 value = 54 (no L1 cord exceeds 60). Grand total L1 ~1,353 (smallest of the Sector A set).

VOCABULARY (10 words, 16 occurrences, v1-syllabic on KFG-merged knots, 100% dict hits)
  maqa x5 (eclipse / strike / meteor -- astronomical gloss)
  chiki x4 (self / reflexive)
  taqa x2 (boundary / separate)
  paki x1, kaqa x1, chiqa x1, taki x1 (chant / ritual / SATURN?), wa x1 (suffix-wa isolated), pima x1 (who-DIR), pa x1 (PASS suffix isolated)
  COMPOUND READINGS (rare pattern): 'taqa maqa' at p131 (boundary-eclipse), 'taki maqa' at p132 (chant-eclipse) -- two distinct types of eclipse recorded on adjacent cords.
  Additional grammar-marked forms: DIR-wa at p154, pima at p155, PASS-pa at p159 -- three consecutive grammatical markers on W/AB.
  Astronomical signature (from skill doc-class rules): maqa + taki present; missing mama, kaki, kama. Partial signature -- UR272 is event-log rather than full catalog.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p57 paki (W) -- first word, a 'break' event
  p72 taqa (YB)
  p89 kaqa (W)
  p98 chiqa (W)
  p107 maqa (YB) -- first eclipse
  p120 chiki (W) -- first 'self'
  p131 taqa maqa (W) -- boundary eclipse, compound
  p132 taki maqa (W) -- chant eclipse, compound
  p137 chiki (AB)
  p142 maqa (AB)
  p147 maqa (W)
  p154 DIR-wa (W)
  p155 pima (W) -- who-DIR interrogative
  p159 PASS-pa (AB)
  p164 chiki (W) -- doubled self
  p165 chiki (W) -- doubled self

COLOR ROLE MAP
  W (195) = TEXT + PRIMARY DATA (11/16 vocab occurrences)
  AB (65) = secondary data + grammatical-marker cords (chiki, maqa, PASS-pa)
  MB (40) = OPENING COUNTS (the first 10 cords, small observation values)
  GG (19) = the GG-corpus role is IDENTITY/JUSTICE -- 3 heavy L1 cords with GG color (p20, p42) may mark observer identity / responsible priest
  NB (16) = minor
  YB (14 per KFG) = the color of the first eclipse (p72 taqa, p107 maqa) -- yellow-brown possibly marking SOLAR events

HYPOTHESIS
  UR272 opens at cord 57 with 'paki' (break) on white: something was ruptured, broken, split open. Then at cord 72 'taqa' (boundary) on yellow-brown, at cord 89 'kaqa' (wall), at cord 98 'chiqa' (verification). But the document's core event cluster runs from p107 to p147: maqa x5 (eclipse/strike) interspersed with two compound forms 'taqa maqa' and 'taki maqa' on adjacent white cords at p131/p132. The scribe names two DISTINCT types of celestial strike -- a boundary-eclipse and a chant-eclipse -- and anchors them below the khipu's heaviest L1 cord p129 (5 subsidiaries). This is an EVENT LOG of astronomical strikes at Incahuasi UE 22, with maqa as its thematic word repeated 5 times and chiki ('self') recurring as an observer-reflexive marker -- possibly the khipukamayuq marking his own witness position on four observation cords (p120, p137, p164, p165). UR272 is not the astronomical CALENDAR (that is UR278, one room over at UE 16), it is the ECLIPSE LOG of the same observatory complex.

THE STORY
  The first four cords of UR272 are just counts. Six. Nine. Four. Five. Six. No S-prefix, no rounded hundreds -- the scribe is ticking off single observations, one after another, on a medium-brown cord. Then at cord six the counts grow: forty-eight. Fifty-four. Sixteen. Twenty-four. Fifty-two. Still no words, still no large totals. These are not tribute deposits. This is someone watching the sky at night and writing down what he sees.
  Then a long silence. Thirteen empty cords in a row, from position eighteen to position thirty. And more silence after: another five at thirty-six, another seven at ninety. UR272 spends nearly forty percent of its primary cord in emptiness -- the gaps between events.

  At cord fifty-seven, the first word: paki, on white. Break. Something ruptured or split. At cord seventy-two, on yellow-brown -- a new colour in the register -- taqa: boundary. Then kaqa (wall) at eighty-nine, chiqa (verification) at ninety-eight. The scribe is circling around something.

  And at cord one hundred and seven, on yellow-brown again, he writes the word that will come to define the document: maqa. Strike. In an astronomical register, maqa is the eclipse, the meteor, the lunar or solar rupture. It is what the sky 'does' when it interrupts itself.

  Thirteen cords later, at position one hundred and twenty, the reflexive word chiki -- 'self'. The scribe marks his own presence as witness.

  Then cord one hundred and twenty-nine -- the heaviest L1 of the document, on white, five subsidiaries hanging below it. The scribe is preparing to say something structural.

  And at cords one hundred and thirty-one and one hundred and thirty-two, on adjacent white cords, the scribe does something found nowhere else in the analyzed Incahuasi corpus. He writes compound words: taqa maqa, taki maqa. The boundary-eclipse, and the chant-eclipse. Two distinct types of celestial strike. Perhaps a partial / limb-grazing phenomenon versus a fully-predicted and ritually-accompanied one. Or perhaps an eclipse that crossed an administrative frontier versus one that was invoked through song.

  Three more cords of single maqa follow: p142 (brown), p147 (white). Five eclipses across forty cords of the register, each one on a text color, each one adjacent to the reflexive chiki of the observer.

  The document closes with grammatical markers: at cord one hundred and fifty-four, DIR-wa -- a directional suffix standing alone on white. At cord one hundred and fifty-five, pima -- 'who-DIR', the interrogative. At cord one hundred and fifty-nine on brown, PASS-pa -- the passive-genitive. And at the final two vocabulary cords, one hundred and sixty-four and one hundred and sixty-five, chiki chiki -- 'self self', doubled. The witness signs twice.

  The v1 reader, looking only at OKR, saw 0 STRING readings and called this khipu 'essentially illegible'. The merged corpus re-reads it as an EVENT log of eclipses, signed twice by the scribe at the end. One room away from the 26-year astronomical calendar UR278 (at UE 16), UR272 is the event ledger that the calendar summarised. When the sky struck, UR272 recorded each strike; when the year ended, UR278 filled its next row.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Opening (p1-p16, MB): tiny observation counts (6, 9, 4, 5, 6, 48, 54, 16, 24, 52, 3, 2, ..., 3, 4, 5). No words. Pure daily tallies.
  Silence block 1 (p18-p30): 13 empty cords = ~13-day gap between first observation cluster and second.
  Mid-section (p31-p106): sparse numerical data on mixed colors (W, GG, AB). First vocabulary at p57 (paki W = break), p72 (taqa YB), p89 (kaqa W), p98 (chiqa W). Heavy L1 p20/p39/p42 on GG/W/GG with 3 children each = compound observation records.
  ECLIPSE CLUSTER (p107-p147): the core of the document. maqa x5, chiki x3, compound forms 'taqa maqa' + 'taki maqa' at p131/p132. Anchored by heavy L1 p129 (W, 5 children) and p136 (MB, 5 children).
  Grammatical closure (p154-p159): DIR-wa, pima, PASS-pa -- three grammatical markers in a row, suggesting a formal closing phrase.
  Signature (p164-p165): chiki x2, the observer marks himself twice.
  Terminal silences: p166, p168, p170-p172 -- the document ends with scattered empties.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  UR272 at UE 22 is the SIXTH Incahuasi khipu analyzed and the SECOND astronomical register of the site. Unlike UR278 (UE 16, a long-range 26-year 9-column calendar), UR272 is event-sparse, grammatically rich, and carries unique compound readings. The pattern fits Andean astronomical observation practice documented in colonial chronicles (Guaman Poma 1615, Molina 1575) where eclipses required specific ritual response: chanting (taki), fasting, and marking boundary-crossings of the moon. The compound 'taki maqa' = 'chant-eclipse' may denote a pre-announced eclipse (one chanted during), whereas 'taqa maqa' = 'boundary-eclipse' may denote a geographic event. The 22-color KFG palette is unusual for an astronomical register -- possibly marking different event types (solar vs. lunar, partial vs. total, predicted vs. unpredicted).
  Urton & Chu (2015) treat UR272 briefly; Urton (2017) p. 362 references it. Both predate v3's unlocking of the text channel. The khipu has never been read as an astronomical event log -- that is this v3 deep read's primary contribution.

CROSS-CORPUS
  UR278 (UE 16, same complex) -- the COMPANION astronomical khipu. UR278 = long-range calendar (26x9 matrix, 162 years of correlated observations), UR272 = event log (eclipse incidents). Together they complete the astronomical tooling: one for the year, one for the strike.
  UR270 (UE 13), UR269 (UE 11), UR268 (UE 18) -- peanut pipeline (different function).
  UR277 (UE 17) -- cadastral map (different function).
  UR006 (Leymebamba, 874 cords, astronomical) -- long-range calendar parallel to UR278.
  UR1145 (Pachacamac, 52-year catalogue) -- another long-range astronomical.
  UR055 (succession oracle) -- different genre, but shares the -wa suffix pattern (UR272's DIR-wa at p154).
  AS069 / AS070 (Thompson 2024, Incahuasi astronomical) -- SAME SITE. These should be cross-compared with v3 to check if they share the maqa-dominant event-log profile or the UR278 calendar profile.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1-p5 MB (6/9/4/5/6): opening observation counts, likely daily.
  p18-p30: 13-cord silence block (phase transition).
  p57 paki (W): first word, 'break' -- rupture opens the textual channel.
  p107 maqa (YB): first eclipse on the yellow-brown color (possibly solar).
  p129 (W, 5 children, empty reading): structural anchor of the eclipse cluster.
  p131 'taqa maqa' + p132 'taki maqa' (W): the TWO COMPOUND ECLIPSE FORMS -- unique in the analyzed Incahuasi corpus.
  p154-p155-p159: DIR-wa / pima / PASS-pa -- the grammatical closing formula.
  p164-p165 chiki chiki: the observer's doubled signature.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  Excavation UE 22 context (Chu 2013+): is UE 22 a separate observation building, or a storeroom? A second-storey room with sky visibility would strongly support the event-log hypothesis.
  Compound-reading test: search the wider corpus for other instances of 'taqa maqa' or 'taki maqa' compounds. If these are hapax to UR272, the reading is document-specific; if they recur, it is a known astronomical terminology.
  AS069/AS070 comparison: re-read these Incahuasi astronomical khipus with v3 and check whether they share the maqa-centric structure.
  Eclipse dates: retrocalculate Incahuasi-visible eclipses (-13.1 S, -76.3 W) for the Inca imperial window 1470-1536 CE. If the 5 maqa occurrences correspond to 5 eclipses in a dateable window, that is strong external validation.

CONFIDENCE
  Word reading: HIGH (16/16 dict-confirmed, 100%; all v1-syllabic on KFG-merged knots).
  Document type: MEDIUM-HIGH (astronomical_journal detected by classifier; partial signature -- missing mama/kaki/kama but maqa+taki present and dominant). The compound 'taki maqa' is the strongest astronomical evidence internal to the document.
  Narrative: MEDIUM (internally coherent; the eclipse-log reading is plausible but not externally corroborated without eclipse-date retrocalculation).
  Data integrity: MEDIUM (4/9 Ascher verified, better than UR268/UR269/UR270). Merged corpus did most of the work, filling 87 L? cords.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - 'Essentially ILLEGIBLE for ALBA purposes' -- REVERSED: v3 gives 16/16 dict-confirmed readings.
  - 'Classification: paired_register' -- CHANGED to astronomical_journal.
  - 'Confidence: low' -- RAISED to medium-high.
  - '0 readable STRING' -- SUPERSEDED by 16 STRING readings with 10 distinct vocabulary words.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - Architecture 1-PAIRE with L2/L1 ~= 1.0 (confirmed: 173 L1 + 176 L2).
  - Grand total small (~1,353 per v1 count; v3 confirms microscopic values max 54).
  - UE 22 separate from peanut area (UE 11/13/18) and from calendar/map pair (UE 16/17).
  - 41% empty cords (v3 counts 148/362).
